message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>As long as there is no vaccine available, having access to inhibitors of SARS-CoV-2 will be of utmost importance. Antivirals against coronaviruses do not exist, hence global drug re-purposing efforts have been carried out to identify agents that may provide clinical benefit to patients with COVID-19. Itraconazole, an antifungal agent, has been reported to have potential activity against animal coronaviruses.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Using cell-based phenotypic assays, the
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    antiviral activity of itraconazole and 17-OH itraconazole was assessed against clinical isolates from a German and Belgian patient infected with SARS-CoV-2.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Itraconazole demonstrated antiviral activity in human Caco-2 cells (EC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    = 2.3 μM; MTT assay). Similarly, its primary metabolite, 17-OH itraconazole, showed inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 activity (EC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    = 3.6 μM). Remdesivir inhibited viral replication with an EC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    = 0.4 μM. Itraconazole and 17-OH itraconazole resulted in a viral yield reduction
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    of approximately 2-log
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    and approximately 1-log
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    , as measured in both Caco-2 cells and VeroE6-eGFP cells, respectively. The viral yield reduction brought about by remdesivir or GS-441524 (parent nucleoside of the antiviral prodrug remdesivir; positive control) was more pronounced, with an approximately 3 log
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    drop and >4 log
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    drop in Caco-2 cells and VeroE6-eGFP cells, respectively.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Discussion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Itraconazole and 17-OH itraconazole exert
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    low micromolar activity against SARS-CoV-2. Despite the
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    antiviral activity, itraconazole did not result in a beneficial effect in hospitalized COVID-19 patients in a clinical study (EudraCT Number: 2020-001243-15).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        Itraconazole exerted
                        <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                        low micromolar activity against SARS-CoV-2 (EC
                        <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                        = 2.3 μM)
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Remdesivir demonstrated potent antiviral activity, confirming validity of the assay</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Itraconazole has since shown no efficacy in a clinical study in hospitalized COVID-19 patients</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: In Vitro Activity of Itraconazole Against SARS-CoV-2
